text: The surgeon positions the patient with the head on the left side and places the optic on the rectus abdominis muscle line, using a zero-degree lens. The surgical setup includes a triangle configuration for instrument access, with the optic, suction, bipolar, and scissors arranged for optimal use. The surgeon frees the spleen, tail of the pancreas, and colon in one block, checking for adhesions.

The surgeon continues the dissection, clearly visualizing the tail of the pancreas and adrenal glands. The surgical field includes the colon, which is being prepared for further dissection. The kidney appears non-existent, resembling lipomectomy rather than nephrectomy, complicating localization. The surgeon prefers a transperitoneal approach due to potential adherence issues.

The surgeon incises external to the gonadal vein, making a horizontal incision and vertically under the low pole of the kidney, although the low pole is not clearly visible due to the kidney's phantomatic appearance. A parallel vein in the mesocolon is identified, distinct from the gonadal vein within the genital fascia.

The surgeon navigates around the dilated pelvis of the kidney, attempting to locate the psoas muscle while avoiding direct contact with the kidney hilum. The uterus and the cavity are visible, and the surgeon incises near the lower pole of the kidney, which is difficult to visualize due to its reduced volume.

The surgeon frees the psoas muscle and carefully dissects around the lumbar vein, avoiding injury to the surrounding structures. The adrenal glands are visible and preserved during the dissection. The surgeon approaches the renal vein, using a grasping technique to separate fibrotic tissue around the pedicle, while noting the presence of a node and minimal inflammation.

The surgeon encounters fibrotic tissue while dissecting lymphatic tissue containing small vessels. Using scissors and bipolar graspers, the surgeon identifies a small artery, placing a purple Hem-o-lok clip to secure it. The tissue is fibrotic, and the kidney is atrophic, making the artery difficult to locate. The clip is applied to ensure safety, as the artery is confirmed to be small.

The surgeon cleans the vein and applies Hem-o-lok clips to secure branches. Nodes are visible behind the vein, and the hilum is positioned downward. The adrenal gland is preserved during the dissection. The surgeon mobilizes the tissue, preparing to remove the kidney through an inguinal incision, ensuring minimal muscle fiber cutting. The incision is made vertically behind the adrenal glands to preserve them while removing surrounding fat tissue.

The surgeon continues dissection to separate a small bridge of tissue containing vessels, taking care to avoid bleeding from the adrenal glands. The diaphragm is visible, and caution is exercised when using monopolar coagulation to prevent diaphragm contraction. The surgeon detaches fat tissue and begins freeing the upper pole of the kidney, while controlling the uterus and identifying the lumbar vein near the renal vein and artery.

The surgeon encounters a small branch while blocked by the patient's arms but proceeds to coagulate a large vein. The kidney is released, and the surgeon incises the lateral peritoneal attachments of the kidney, demonstrating the incision made on the upper pole. The procedure involves traction and coagulation of vessels to complete the kidney removal.

The surgeon prepares to remove the kidney by making an incision in the inguinal area, avoiding cutting muscle fibers. A bag is used to ensure all kidney tissue is removed without leaving any pieces inside. The kidney is positioned anatomically before placing it in the bag.

The surgeon uses the Parkinson technique to shake the kidney into the retrieval bag. The kidney is then placed in the bag, and the surgeon ensures the gas is still inside before placing a drain. The muscle fibers are split without incision to remove the kidney through the inguinal incision. The procedure concludes with no bleeding and successful control of the pedicle.
